questions.question:
The CEO's decision to quit was as shocking to the Board as it was to ______.

A. I
B. me
C. my
D. myself
questions.to_find: correct pronoun to fill the blank in the comparative structure

understand.plain: The sentence uses a comparison: 'as shocking to X as it was to ___'. The blank follows the preposition 'to', which demands an object pronoun. In English, prepositions are always followed by object (accusative) forms of pronouns, not subject forms.

keywords.explain: Pronoun used as the object of a verb or preposition (me, him, her, us, them).
keywords.example: 'Give it to me.' — 'me' is the object of the preposition 'to'.

keywords.explain: Pronoun used as the subject of a verb (I, he, she, we, they).
keywords.example: 'I decided to quit.' — 'I' is the subject.

keywords.explain: Pronoun ending in -self/-selves, used when the subject and object refer to the same entity, or for emphasis.
keywords.example: 'She hurt herself.' — refers back to the subject.

understand.visual_alt: No figure required for this grammar question.

given.aim: Select the grammatically correct pronoun for the blank after the preposition 'to'

terms.meaning: A preposition that links a noun or pronoun to the rest of the sentence; it requires an object pronoun after it.
terms.example: 'It was shocking to him.' — 'him' is the object pronoun after 'to'.
terms.connects: Determines which pronoun case (object) must follow.

terms.meaning: A structure comparing two entities; both sides of the comparison are grammatically parallel.
terms.example: 'It was shocking to the Board as it was to [pronoun].' — both slots take the same grammatical role.
terms.connects: The blank plays the same role as 'the Board', namely the object of a preposition.

terms.meaning: English pronouns change form based on their grammatical role: subject (I), object (me), possessive (my), reflexive (myself).
terms.example: 'He told me.' vs 'I told him.' — form depends on whether the pronoun is subject or object.
terms.connects: Only the object form 'me' can correctly follow a preposition.

given.plan: Identify the grammatical role of the blank (object of the preposition 'to'), then choose the object-case pronoun.

steps.title: Identify the blank's grammatical role
steps.apply: The blank appears after the preposition 'to' in the phrase '...as it was to ____'. Any pronoun following a preposition must be in the object case.
steps.note: Prepositions govern nouns/pronouns in the object case in English.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: Option A ('I') is a subject pronoun — cannot follow a preposition. Option C ('my') is a possessive adjective — modifies a noun, not used standalone here. Option D ('myself') is a reflexive pronoun — requires a prior referent in the same clause ('it was to myself' lacks one).
steps.note: Reflexive 'myself' is only correct when the subject and object are the same person in the clause.

steps.title: Confirm the correct answer
steps.apply: Option B ('me') is the object pronoun for first person singular, correctly following the preposition 'to'. The sentence reads: '...as it was to me.' — grammatically perfect.
steps.note: The parallel structure confirms: 'shocking to the Board as it was to me' both use object forms after 'to'.

questions.formula_note: This question tests pronoun case selection after a preposition; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
The fisherman, ________ the flood victims owed their lives, were rewarded by the government.

A. whom
B. to which
C. to whom
D. that
questions.to_find: correct relative pronoun phrase to complete the relative clause

understand.plain: The sentence has a relative clause describing 'the fisherman'. The flood victims 'owed their lives' to the fisherman — so the preposition 'to' must be part of the clause. Because the antecedent is a person (the fisherman), 'whom' is required rather than 'which' or 'that'.

keywords.explain: A pronoun (who, whom, which, that) that introduces a relative clause modifying a noun.
keywords.example: 'The man whom I saw' — 'whom' introduces the relative clause modifying 'the man'.

keywords.explain: Object-case relative pronoun used for people; 'who' is subject case, 'whom' is object case.
keywords.example: 'The teacher whom the students admired' — 'whom' is the object of 'admired'.

keywords.explain: When a relative pronoun is the object of a preposition, the preposition can either precede the pronoun (fronted: 'to whom') or appear at the end (stranded: 'whom... to'). Fronting is more formal.
keywords.example: 'The charity to which he donated' (fronted) vs 'The charity which he donated to' (stranded).

understand.visual_alt: No figure required for this grammar question.

given.aim: Fill the blank with the correct relative pronoun phrase that links 'the fisherman' to 'owed their lives'

terms.meaning: The full verb phrase; 'owed to' requires an object — the fisherman — and the preposition 'to' must appear in the relative clause.
terms.example: 'They owed their lives to the fisherman.' => relative clause: 'to whom the flood victims owed their lives'.
terms.connects: Establishes that 'to' is part of the clause and the pronoun is an object.

terms.meaning: The noun modified by the relative clause; because it refers to a person, 'whom/who' is correct rather than 'which'.
terms.example: 'The fisherman' is a person => use 'whom', not 'which'.
terms.connects: Rules out options B ('to which') since 'which' is for non-persons.

terms.meaning: When the relative pronoun is the object of a verb or preposition in the clause, use 'whom' (not 'who').
terms.example: 'The doctor whom they called' — 'whom' is the object of 'called'.
terms.connects: Rules out 'who' or 'that' in formal contexts where the pronoun follows a preposition.

given.plan: Reconstruct the underlying sentence, identify that 'to' is needed and the pronoun refers to a person, then select 'to whom'.

steps.title: Reconstruct the underlying idea
steps.apply: Base sentence: 'The flood victims owed their lives to the fisherman.' The relative clause replaces 'the fisherman' and must carry the preposition: '...to whom the flood victims owed their lives...'
steps.note: The preposition 'to' is essential; dropping it produces an ungrammatical clause ('whom the flood victims owed their lives' is incomplete without 'to').

steps.title: Check person vs thing
steps.apply: The antecedent is 'the fisherman' (a person). For persons, use 'who/whom', not 'which'. This eliminates option B ('to which').
steps.note: 'Which' is for non-human nouns; 'that' can be used for persons but not comfortably with a fronted preposition.

steps.title: Choose subject vs object pronoun
steps.apply: In the relative clause, the fisherman is the object of the preposition 'to', so the object form 'whom' is required (not 'who'). Therefore 'to whom' is correct.
steps.note: Option A ('whom' alone) omits the necessary preposition 'to', making the clause grammatically incomplete.

questions.formula_note: This question tests relative pronoun selection (person vs thing, subject vs object) and preposition placement in a relative clause.

questions.question:
Which one of the following sentence sequences creates a coherent narrative?

i. Once on the terrace, on her way to her small room in the corner, she notices the man right away.
ii. She begins to pant by the time she has climbed all the stairs.
iii. Mina has bought vegetables and rice at the market, so her bag is heavy.
iv. He was leaning against the parapet, watching the traffic below.

A. (ii), (i), (iii), (iv)
B. (iii), (ii), (i), (iv)
C. (i), (iii), (ii), (iv)
D. (iii), (ii), (iv), (i)
questions.to_find: correct ordering of the four sentences to form a coherent narrative

understand.plain: The four sentences describe Mina returning from the market and noticing a man on the terrace. A coherent narrative must follow logical temporal and spatial sequence: first she is at the market with heavy bags, then she climbs the stairs (panting), then she reaches the terrace, and finally she notices a man leaning against the parapet.

keywords.explain: A sequence of sentences that flows logically in time and space, with each sentence connecting naturally to the next.
keywords.example: Events described in chronological order with pronoun references that make sense given what has been introduced.

keywords.explain: Words or phrases signalling the time sequence between events (e.g., 'by the time', 'once on the terrace').
keywords.example: 'Once on the terrace' implies she has already climbed the stairs before this sentence.

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; the question involves sentence ordering.

given.aim: Arrange the four sentences in a sequence that produces a logically connected story about Mina

terms.meaning: Sets up the initial situation: Mina has just been to the market and her bag is heavy — this is the starting point.
terms.example: This must come first because it introduces Mina and the context for why she would be tired.
terms.connects: Explains the cause of panting (heavy bag + stairs) in sentence ii.

terms.meaning: Describes Mina climbing the stairs with effort, a direct consequence of carrying a heavy bag.
terms.example: Follows naturally from iii because we know her bag is heavy.
terms.connects: Leads logically to sentence i where she arrives on the terrace.

terms.meaning: Once on the terrace, she notices 'the man' — the definite article 'the' implies the man must have been introduced or described before this sentence.
terms.example: The man is first described in sentence iv (leaning against the parapet), so iv must precede i.
terms.connects: Sentence iv must come before i because 'the man' in i is a back-reference.

given.plan: Use pronoun/article reference tracking and temporal connectors to fix the order: iii -> ii -> iv -> i.

steps.title: Identify the opening sentence
steps.apply: Sentence iii introduces Mina and explains she has a heavy bag from the market. No prior context is needed. This must be first.
steps.note: Starting with iii anchors the narrative in a concrete situation.

steps.title: Sequence using causal and temporal logic
steps.apply: Because her bag is heavy (iii), she pants while climbing the stairs (ii). After reaching the top, she is on the terrace (i). Sentence ii must come before i since 'Once on the terrace' in i implies stairs have already been climbed.
steps.note: 'By the time she has climbed all the stairs' in ii directly precedes 'Once on the terrace' in i.

steps.title: Resolve the definite article 'the man'
steps.apply: In sentence i, 'she notices the man right away' uses the definite article 'the', implying the man has already been described. Sentence iv describes him — 'He was leaning against the parapet' — so iv must precede i. Order so far: iii, ii, iv, i.
steps.note: Definite article 'the man' in i requires prior introduction of the man; sentence iv provides that description.

steps.title: Verify option D
steps.apply: Option D gives (iii), (ii), (iv), (i). Check: Market bag (iii) -> panting on stairs (ii) -> man leaning on parapet (iv) -> notices man on terrace (i). This reads as a seamless narrative.
steps.note: All other options break either the temporal flow or the pronoun/article reference chain.

questions.formula_note: Sentence ordering is solved by tracking temporal connectors, causal links, and definite-article back-references to determine the unique coherent sequence.

questions.question:
Sequence the following sentences in a coherent passage.

P: This fortuitous geological event generated a colossal amount of energy and heat that resulted in the rocks rising to an average height of 4 km across the contact zone.
Q: Thus, the geophysicists tend to think of the Himalayas as an active geological feature rather than a static geological feature.
R: The natural process of the cooling of magma absorbed a massive quantity of atmospheric carbon dioxide, altering the earth's atmosphere and making it better suited for life.
S: Many millennia ago, a breakaway chunk of bedrock from the Antarctic Plate collided with the massive Eurasian Plate.

A. QPSR
B. SPRQ
C. SRPQ
D. SRBQ
questions.to_find: correct sequence of sentences S, P, Q, R to form a coherent geological passage

understand.plain: The passage describes a geological collision event and its consequences. Sentence S introduces the event (Antarctic Plate colliding with Eurasian Plate). Sentence P describes the immediate physical result (rocks rising). Sentence R describes the atmospheric consequence (carbon dioxide absorption). Sentence Q presents the scientific conclusion about the Himalayas.

keywords.explain: In sentence P, 'this fortuitous geological event' refers back to the collision described in sentence S — a demonstrative back-reference confirming S precedes P.
keywords.example: 'This event' can only make sense if the event has already been introduced.

keywords.explain: The word 'thus' in sentence Q signals a conclusion drawn from prior information, so Q must come last.
keywords.example: 'Thus, scientists think...' — this is a summary/conclusion statement.

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; this question involves logical sequencing of a science passage.

given.aim: Order sentences S, P, R, Q to form a logically connected paragraph about the Himalayan geological formation

terms.meaning: Introduces the initial event — a chunk of bedrock from the Antarctic Plate colliding with the Eurasian Plate — which is the cause of everything else.
terms.example: Must come first because all other sentences refer back to consequences of this collision.
terms.connects: The word 'this fortuitous event' in P is a direct back-reference to S.

terms.meaning: Describes the immediate physical consequence of the collision: energy and heat causing rocks to rise.
terms.example: Follows S because 'this fortuitous geological event' in P refers to the collision in S.
terms.connects: Sets up the geological context for R and Q.

terms.meaning: Uses 'thus' — a concluding discourse marker — to state the scientific inference, so it must come last.
terms.example: 'Thus, the geophysicists tend to think...' is a concluding generalisation.
terms.connects: Draws upon all prior sentences to reach a conclusion.

given.plan: Fix S as first (introduces the event), P as second (immediate result via back-reference), then decide between R and Q; Q uses 'thus' so it must be last, placing R third.

steps.title: Identify the opening sentence using reference analysis
steps.apply: Sentence S introduces a past event with no prior context needed: 'Many millennia ago, a breakaway chunk of bedrock... collided...'. This is the initiating event. Sentence S must come first.
steps.note: No demonstrative or anaphoric reference in S; it introduces everything fresh.

steps.title: Place P second via back-reference
steps.apply: Sentence P opens with 'This fortuitous geological event' — the demonstrative 'this' refers to the collision described in S. So P immediately follows S.
steps.note: Demonstrative pronouns/adjectives always follow their referent.

steps.title: Determine the position of R
steps.apply: Sentence R describes 'the natural process of the cooling of magma' absorbing carbon dioxide — a secondary process that follows the initial energy release described in P. R comes after P as a continuation of the geological aftermath.
steps.note: The cooling of magma is a consequence of the heat described in P, so R naturally follows P.

steps.title: Place Q last as the conclusion
steps.apply: Sentence Q starts with 'Thus' — a logical conclusion marker. It synthesises everything before it to state that geophysicists see the Himalayas as an active feature. Q is the final sentence.
steps.note: Conclusion sentences using 'thus', 'therefore', or 'hence' are almost always placed last in GATE reading-passage ordering questions.

questions.formula_note: Sentence ordering is resolved by identifying back-references (demonstratives), causal chain logic, and the position of discourse-conclusion markers such as 'thus'.

questions.question:
Sequence the following sentences (P, Q, R, S) in a coherent passage:

P: Shifu's student exclaimed, "Why do you run since the bull is an illusion?"
Q: Shifu said, "Surely my running away from the bull is also an illusion."
R: One day, when a bull began to chase Shifu, he began to run for his life.
S: Many millennia ago, when a bull began to chase Shifu, he began to run for his life.

A. SRPQ
B. SRPQ
C. RSPQ
D. RPQS
questions.to_find: correct ordering of sentences R, S, P, Q to form a coherent narrative about Shifu and the bull

understand.plain: The passage is a philosophical anecdote about illusion. A bull chases Shifu; a student questions why he runs if the bull is an illusion; Shifu responds that his running is equally an illusion. The narrative must flow: event -> student's question -> Shifu's response.

keywords.explain: A brief story with a setup (the event), a complication (the question), and a resolution (the insight/punchline).
keywords.example: Setup: Shifu runs from bull. Complication: student questions this. Resolution: Shifu's witty reply.

keywords.explain: Sentences using 'he', 'his', or 'the bull' assume prior introduction of these referents.
keywords.example: Sentence P refers to 'the bull' and 'you' (Shifu) — both must already be introduced before P.

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; this is a sentence-ordering passage about Shifu.

given.aim: Order R, S, P, Q so that the anecdote about Shifu and the illusory bull is logically and narratively coherent

terms.meaning: One day a bull chases Shifu and he runs for his life — the initiating event of the story.
terms.example: Introduces both Shifu and the bull chase; must be first to set up everything else.
terms.connects: P and Q both refer to 'the bull' and 'running', which R establishes.

terms.meaning: S provides the philosophical premise (the bull is an illusion) or re-sets the scene, following the initial chase.
terms.example: Follows R by expanding or qualifying the situation before the dialogue starts.
terms.connects: The student's question in P ('Why do you run since the bull is an illusion?') requires S to have stated the bull is an illusion.

terms.meaning: Shifu's reply — 'my running is also an illusion' — is the resolution and must come last as the punchline.
terms.example: 'Surely my running away... is also an illusion.' This is a logical response to P's question, so Q must follow P.
terms.connects: Q is the philosophical conclusion; it logically follows P's question.

given.plan: R introduces the event, S introduces the philosophical premise about illusion, P poses the student's question, Q delivers Shifu's answer. Order: R, S, P, Q.

steps.title: Fix R as the opening
steps.apply: Sentence R states: 'One day, when a bull began to chase Shifu, he began to run for his life.' This introduces all characters and the event. No prior context is assumed. R must come first.
steps.note: The temporal marker 'One day' and the introduction of Shifu and the bull confirm R is the narrative's opening.

steps.title: Place S after R
steps.apply: Sentence S introduces the philosophical observation — that the bull is (or may be) an illusion. The student's question in P ('Why do you run since the bull is an illusion?') presupposes this idea. Therefore S must precede P.
steps.note: P uses 'since the bull is an illusion' as a given fact; S must have established this before P.

steps.title: Place P as the student's question
steps.apply: With R and S in place, P naturally follows as the student challenges Shifu: 'Why do you run since the bull is an illusion?' This is a reaction to both the running (R) and the philosophical frame (S).
steps.note: P contains a direct quotation — a question — which demands a response from Shifu.

steps.title: Confirm Q is the conclusion/punchline
steps.apply: Sentence Q is Shifu's reply: 'Surely my running away from the bull is also an illusion.' This is the punchline, resolving P's question. Q must be last. Final order: R, S, P, Q — matching option C.
steps.note: The word 'also' in Q explicitly responds to P's point about the bull being an illusion, confirming Q follows P.

questions.formula_note: Narrative ordering is determined by tracking character introductions, pronoun back-references, premise-before-question logic, and the question-answer structure of dialogue.
